who has the right of way in international waters Both vessels have the right of way in international waters, with no specific vessel being required to give way to another under international maritime law, unless a specific situation dictates otherwise. This principle ensures that all vessels maintain equal responsibility and rights when navigating open seas.

who has the right of way in international waters Both vessels have the right of way in international waters, as there are no specific rules assigning priority to one vessel over another in the open sea.

who has the right of way in international waters Both vessels have the right of way in international waters, as no specific vessel is designated as having priority in the absence of defined circumstances or hazards. This principle ensures that all vessels maintain equal responsibility and rights on open seas.

who has the right of way in international waters Both vessels have the right of way in international waters, with no vessel having precedence over another unless specific circumstances dictate otherwise, as defined by international maritime law and regulations.

who has the right of way in international waters Both vessels have the right of way in international waters unless specific conditions or situations dictate otherwise, such as the presence of a larger or more maneuverable ship that could better avoid a collision. In the absence of such conditions, both vessels are equally responsible for maintaining a safe distance and course.
